conversation.content:
Web search returned nothing useful. Let me note the structural principle from established amyloid kinetics theory that I already know, and finalize from local evidence.

**Key insight from the Knowles/Cohen/Meisl master equation:** Amyloid mass conversion follows `dc/dt = (capacity)·[primary nucleation + autocatalytic growth]`. The lag phase arises because at `c≈0`, only the weak primary-nucleation source operates; once a small amount of aggregate exists, secondary nucleation (∝ c² or saturating Hill) ignites exponentially. The 0.8941 ancestor captures all this. The remaining error is **shape loss** (slope/half-time timing), worst on cross-protein eval-only data.

The best ancestors all scored 0.8941 — a hard plateau. The structural change with clearest payoff: the onset term `c8²·time` is **linear in time**, which over-drives onset at late time even though capacity gates it. A physically better lag generator is **primary nucleation proportional to remaining monomer** (mass action), i.

h early ∝time behavior but stops over-injecting conversion at late times (when capacity is small but nonzero), which was an unphysical drive in the linear-ramp ancestors. This directly targets the dominant held-out/cross-protein **shape (timing) loss** without destabilizing fitting.
- Numerically safe: `time ≥ 0` ⟹ denominator `≥ 1`, bounded in `[0,1)`, no singularities, no fractional/negative powers, 11 constants (< 13). The whole RHS remains a smooth rational function gated by `(plateau − c)`.
